session_metadata.title: Statement of Faith — God the Father
session_metadata.session_topic: Statement of Faith article on God the Father: His identity within the Trinity, eternal nature, role as all-powerful Creator and sustainer, sovereign ordering of all things for His glory, sending of the Son for redemption, and adoption of believers as His children.

theological_themes: creation ex nihilo by the Father
doxology — all things for God's glory
mission of the Son sent by the Father
election before the foundation of the world

chunks.content: We believe that God the Father is the one God — the Father from whom are all things and for whom we live (1 Cor 8:6); the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly realms in Christ (Eph 1:3); one God and Father of all, who is over all and through all and in all (Eph 4:6).
claims: There is one God, the Father, from whom all things originate and for whom believers live.
God the Father is the Father of the Lord Jesus Christ.
The Father is over all, through all, and in all.

chunks.content: God the Father is eternal — He is over all and through all and in all (Eph 4:6).
claims: God the Father is eternal.

chunks.content: God the Father is the all-powerful Creator. He has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly realms in Christ (Eph 1:3); He is one God and Father of all, over all and through all and in all (Eph 4:6); He has created all things, the mystery hidden for ages in God who has created all things (Eph 3:9); for from Him and through Him and to Him are all things — to Him be glory forever! Amen (Rom 11:36).
themes: creation ex nihilo by the Father
doxology — all things for God's glory
claims: God the Father is all-powerful.
God the Father is the Creator of all things.
All things are from Him, through Him, and to Him.

chunks.content: God the Father is the sustainer of the universe — from whom are all things and for whom we live (1 Cor 8:6); the One who accomplishes all things according to the counsel of His will (Eph 1:11); who is over all and through all and in all (Eph 4:6); the Father is the gardener (John 15:1).
claims: God the Father sustains the universe.
He accomplishes all things according to the counsel of His will.
He cultivates and tends His people as the gardener tends the vine.

chunks.content: God the Father has ordered all things according to His perfect will. He predestined believers to adoption as His legal heirs through Jesus Christ, according to the pleasure of His will (Eph 1:5); He has predestined according to the purpose of Him who accomplishes all things according to the counsel of His will (Eph 1:11); His secret plan, the mystery hidden for ages in God who has created all things, is now being made known (Eph 3:9).
themes: creation ex nihilo by the Father
claims: God the Father has ordered all things according to His perfect will.
He predestined believers to adoption as legal heirs through Jesus Christ.
His sovereign decree governs all things according to the counsel of His will.
There is a hidden mystery / secret plan now being disclosed.

chunks.content: God the Father has done all this for His glory — for there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we live (1 Cor 8:6); for from Him and through Him and to Him are all things — to Him be glory forever! Amen (Rom 11:36).
themes: doxology — all things for God's glory
claims: All things exist for the glory of God the Father.
Glory is rightly ascribed to Him forever.

chunks.content: God the Father has sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into this world to redeem sinful man.
themes: mission of the Son sent by the Father
claims: God the Father sent His Son Jesus Christ into the world.
The purpose of the Son's coming is to redeem sinful humanity.

chunks.content: In addition to His role as Father within the Godhead, God the Father adopts as His children all who come to Him in Christ; therefore all believers have the right to address Him as Abba Father. To all who have received Him — those who believe in His name — He has given the right to become God's children (John 1:12). All who are led by the Spirit of God are the sons of God (Rom 8:14). Believers did not receive the spirit of slavery leading again to fear, but received the Spirit of adoption, by whom we cry, 'Abba, Father' (Rom 8:15). 'I will be a father to you, and you will be my sons and daughters,' says the All-Powerful Lord (2 Cor 6:18). Christ came to redeem those who were under the law, so that we may be adopted as sons with full rights (Gal 4:5). He chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world that we should be holy and blameless before Him in love (Eph 1:4). He did this by predestining us to adoption as His legal heirs through Jesus Christ, according to the pleasure of His will (Eph 1:5). In Christ we too have been claimed as God's own possession, since we were predestined according to the purpose of Him who accomplishes all things according to the counsel of His will (Eph 1:11).
themes: election before the foundation of the world
claims: Distinct from His eternal role as Father within the Godhead, God the Father adopts believers as His children.
Adoption is granted to all who come to the Father through Christ / believe in His name.
Adopted believers have the right to address God as 'Abba, Father.'
Adoption is mediated by the Spirit of adoption, not the spirit of slavery.
Believers are made legal heirs through Jesus Christ.
Election to adoption was made in Christ before the foundation of the world.
Believers are God's own possession, claimed according to His sovereign purpose.

chunks.content: Verses cited in support of this article: John 1:12; Romans 8:14-15; Romans 11:36; 1 Corinthians 8:6; 2 Corinthians 6:18; Galatians 4:5; Ephesians 1:5-6, 11; Ephesians 4:6; Ephesians 3:9; John 15:1, 8.

Proposed addition (marked 'SHOULD WE ADD' in source — flagged for editorial decision): Adding to the article that God the Father has sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into this world to redeem sinful man. Supporting passages under consideration: John 3:16 — 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life'; 1 John 4:10 — 'In this is love, not that we have loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins'; Hebrews 1:5 — 'For to which of the angels did God ever say, You are my Son, today I have begotten you? Or again, I will be to him a father, and he shall be to me a son?'
themes: mission of the Son sent by the Father
claims: God the Father loved the world and gave His only Son for those who believe.
The Father sent the Son as the propitiation for sins; love originates with God, not with humans.
The Father-Son relationship is uniquely affirmed of Christ over the angels (Heb 1:5).

God the Father

We believe that

- God the Father is the

SHOULD WE ADD

- has sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into this world to redeem sinful man.

parsing_notes: Document is a Statement of Faith (SOF) article on God the Father, structured as a credal statement followed by supporting scripture quotations under each clause. No personal names or organizational identifiers appear in the source — anonymization was therefore not invasive. Source uses the NET Bible-style wording (e.g., 'God's secret plan' in Eph 3:9, 'All-Powerful Lord' in 2 Cor 6:18); preserved as-is in chunks. Final section labeled 'SHOULD WE ADD' is editorial — captured as a separate chunk of type 'question' (chunk 10) so its provisional status is preserved and not conflated with the affirmed credal statements. The verse-list footer (chunk 9) is a bibliographic index, not doctrinal content — no themes/claims attached. Trinitarian distinction is carefully preserved: the Father is identified as one Person within the Godhead alongside the Son and (implicitly) the Spirit, distinct in role (sender, predestiner, adopter) yet one in the divine essence (Eph 4:6 — 'one God'). Note source distinguishes the Father's eternal role within the Godhead from His adoptive role toward believers (chunk 8 opens with 'In addition to His role as Father within the godhead'). Two verse references in the footer ('Eph 1:5-6' and 'John 15:8') were cited in the bibliographic list but not quoted in the body — included in chunk 9 scripture_refs only.
